coredata.dc:title: Improving the quantum yield of nitrogen-doped carbon dots by varying dopant ratios and pH
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Prado, Megan B.
Truong, Nguyen T.
Wanekaya, Adam K.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) were synthesized in a hydrothermal microwave-assisted pyrolysis using citric acid and urea. Differing carbon-to-urea molar ratios were used and the pH-dependent fluorescence and quantum yield (QY) at various pHs were measured. All NCDs demonstrated pH-dependent fluorescence with increased fluorescence from pH 1-13. Overall, the NCDs with a carbon-to-dopant molar ratio of 1:6 demonstrated the highest QY of 19% in pH 11. Additionally, high quantum yields were observed between pH 7-11. Therefore, NCDs, as prepared, may serve as cost-effective fluorescent pH sensors or in bioimaging applications.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the Missouri State University Graduate College and the Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry. Additional support by the Harthcock Chemistry Fellowship (to Megan Prado).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed synthesis of NCD based on citric acid as the carbon source and urea as the dopant source. Precursors undergo polymerization and carbonization in a hydrothermal reaction, forming NCDs with pH-sensitive functional groups on their surface.
a: XPS spectrum of NCD (1:6). b: Deconvoluted XPS spectrum C 1s. c: Deconvoluted XPS spectrum N 1s. d: Deconvoluted XPS spectrum O 1s.
a: TEM image of NCD (1:3). b: TEM image of NCD (1:6). c: TEM image of NCD (1:10).
a: Absorbance spectrum for NCD (6:1). b: Emission spectra for NCD (6:1) excited at 280-400 nm.
Comparison of NCD (6:1), (1:1), (1:3), (1:6) and (1:10) in pH 7 at differing concentrations (left to right: 1000 ppm, 500 ppm, 100 ppm, 1 ppm) in visible light (top) and under 365 nm light (bottom).

Emission spectra of NCD (6:1) at 50 ppm in pH 1-13. Inset images: NCD (6:1) in visible light, top, and 365 nm light, bottom, at 100 ppm (left to right: pH 2, pH 7, pH 13); inset plot of normalized maximum fluorescence intensity versus pH.

Emission spectra of NCD (1:1) at 500 ppb in pH 1-13. Inset images: NCD (1:1) in visible light, top, and 365 nm light, bottom, at 100 ppm (left to right: pH 2, pH 7, pH 13); inset plot of normalized maximum fluorescence intensity versus pH.

Emission spectra of NCD (1:3) at 50 ppb in pH 1-13. Inset images: NCD (1:3) in visible light, top, and 365 nm light, bottom, at 100 ppm (left to right: pH 2, pH 7, pH 13); inset plot of normalized maximum fluorescence intensity versus pH.

Emission spectra of NCD (1:6) at 50 ppb in pH 1-13. Inset images: NCD (1:6) in visible light, top, and 365 nm light, bottom, at 100 ppm (left to right: pH 2, pH 7, pH 13); inset plot of normalized maximum fluorescence intensity versus pH.

Emission spectra of NCD (1:10) at 50 ppb in pH 1-12. Inset images: NCD (1:10) in visible light, top, and 365 nm light, bottom, at 100 ppm (left to right: pH 2, pH 7, pH 13); inset plot of normalized maximum fluorescence intensity versus pH.
Quantum yield of NCD (6:1), (1:1), (1:3), (1:6), and (1:10) at pH 2, 5, 7, 11, and 13. All samples in respective pH buffers.

head.ce:title: Improving the quantum yield of nitrogen-doped carbon dots by varying dopant ratios and pH

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry and Biochemistry Department, Missouri State University, Springfield, MO 65897, United States
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry and Biochemistry Department, Missouri State University, Springfield, MO 65897, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) were synthesized in a hydrothermal microwave-assisted pyrolysis using citric acid and urea. Differing carbon-to-urea molar ratios were used and the pH-dependent fluorescence and quantum yield (QY) at various pHs were measured. All NCDs demonstrated pH-dependent fluorescence with increased fluorescence from pH 1-13. Overall, the NCDs with a carbon-to-dopant molar ratio of 1:6 demonstrated the highest QY of 19% in pH 11. Additionally, high quantum yields were observed between pH 7-11. Therefore, NCDs, as prepared, may serve as cost-effective fluorescent pH sensors or in bioimaging applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a sub-class of carbon nanoparticles with zero-dimensionality [1]. Known for their fluorescence, low-cost, biocompatibility, photostability, and tunable optical properties, CDs are attractive for numerous potential applications, including fluorescent pH sensing [2,3], wearable optoelectronics [4], security labeling [4], bioimaging [5], biosensing [5], and drug delivery [6], among others. Increasingly, CDs are desirable since their application is not as limited by properties of low stability, high toxicity, or fast photobleaching, as other traditional fluorescent species like semiconductor quantum dots may exhibit [7]. It is well documented that CDs from pure raw materials such as citric acid have negligible or low quantum yield values (about 5% or less) compared to the doped carbon dots [8]. Therefore, in order to compete with other materials, CDs may be doped to tune their chemical and optical properties to improve their fluorescence quantum yields (QYs). [2,9].

Carbon dots were first discovered by accident in 2004 when Xu et al. synthesized soot-based single-walled carbon nanotubes, producing fluorescent byproducts [10]. These byproducts, later named “carbon dots” in 2006 by Sun et al. are less than 10 nm in size [11]. CDs may be synthesized in top-down or bottom-up syntheses. Top-down syntheses involve the transformation of graphitic material into lateral nanostructures [12] in methods including arc-discharge, laser ablation, chemical exfoliation, and electrochemical oxidation [7,13]. Bottom-up syntheses, alternatively, carbonize and polymerize small organic precursors upon heating to form CDs and other carbon nanoparticles [7,14]. Bottom-up syntheses, including pyrolysis, microwave-assisted pyrolysis, thermal decomposition, hydrothermal, or solvothermal methods, are popular in current research because of their simplicity and low-cost [7,14].

Doping heteroatomic impurities into the carbon core of CDs produces changes in their electronic structure, altering the surface for increased fluorescence [2] . Heteroatoms to carbon (nitrogen, boron, phosphorous, sulfur, etc.) are introduced for their similar atomic sizes and number of electrons to carbon. Of these, nitrogen is ideal, offering 5 valence electrons to alter the carbon core and additionally forming amine functional groups on the surface contributing to pH-dependent optical properties [2]. Urea is a suitable choice as it is readily available and low-cost. For carbon precursors, CDs have been prepared from almost limitless sources including orange juice [15], sucrose [2], bee pollen [16], glycerol [17], ascorbic acid [18], etc. In recent years, nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) have been increasingly researched, with much focus on using of citric acid and urea. However, while altering synthesis methods and reaction conditions to optimize the fluorescent QY are well reported, the effect pH has on the QY is underreported, as many QY calculations are performed at pH 7.

Herein, NCDs were prepared in a hydrothermal microwave-assisted pyrolysis using citric acid as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen source. The molar carbon-to-dopant source ratios were varied to investigate the effects increased dopant ratios have on the QY. Additionally, pH-dependent fluorescence was demonstrated for each species and the QY was determined at various pHs to further the potential application NCDs may have with in vivo pH sensing.

Citric acid monohydrate was purchased from Flinn Scientific (Batavia, IL) and Urea from Alfa Aesar (Haverhill, MA). Quinine sulfate dihydrate was purchased from Acros Organics (Waltham, MA) and 2-aminopyridine from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). All other chemicals were of reagent grade and used without further purification. All solutions were made with deionized water purified further with an in-house distillation system.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed using a Bruker Alpha II Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (OPUS v. 8.5(SP1)). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was conducted using a ThermoFisher Scientific Quattro S Environmental Scanning Electron Microscope and an FEI Quanta 200 Scanning Electron Microscope with an Oxford Inca II EDS detector for energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), (shown in supplementary information). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using a Physical Electronics PHI 5000 VersaProbe II with monochromatic Al X-Ray. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a JEOL 2100F Scanning Transmission Electron Microscope in TEM mode at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Sizing was achieved using ImageJ of resulting SEM and TEM images. Ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV-vis) was performed using an Agilent Technologies Cary 60 UV-vis spectrometer (Cary WinUV v. 5.10.1016). Fluorescence spectroscopy was conducted using a Horiba QM-8075-21-C spectrofluorometer (Felix GX v.4).

While the exact mechanism of CD synthesis from bottom-up methods is not yet well known, they are considered to form through polymerization and carbonization of carbon and dopant precursors (Fig. 1 ), leading to a combination of products dependent on reaction time and temperature [19]. To determine the highest fluorescent QY, the citric acid to urea molar ratios were varied for each NCD sample including (6:1), (1:1), (1:3), (1:6), and (1:10) ratios. In a typical synthesis for NCD (1:1), 30 mmol of citric acid and 30 mmol of urea are dissolved in 30 ml DDI and transferred into respective Teflon cylindrical microwave vessels. To prevent a majority of products from being largely carbonaceous (temperature >200°C) [19] or largely polymeric (temperature <150°C) [19], NCDs were prepared via a hydrothermal microwave method for 10 minutes at 200°C. The CEM Mars 6 Digestive Microwave was used for all syntheses. The method included a ramp time of 20-25 min, with a hold time of 10 min, followed by a cool-down period.

All samples were gravity filtered followed by 0.2 μm syringe filtration to remove any large insoluble carbonaceous material. Then each sample underwent dialysis with a 3.5 kDa MWCO membrane for three consecutive 24-hour periods, changing exchange liquid after every 24 hours. The resulting samples were lyophilized and stored at room temperature.

All NCD samples were prepared in buffered solutions. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY: 54%) was used in the QY calculations as its emission spectrum overlaps the NCD emission. The QY was calculated based on the comparative method using absorbances and integrated fluorescence intensities of the NCD and standard, respectively.

To better understand the chemical surface of the NCDs, FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS were performed to determine which functional groups were present. A representative FT-IR spectrum of NCD (1:1) is shown in Fig. 2 . Broad absorption bands from 2500-3500 cm−1 demonstrate clear overlapping O-H and N-H stretches in NCD (1:1). Additionally, C=O stretches are shown around ∼1680 cm−1, N-H stretches at ∼1550 cm−1, and C-N stretches at ∼1400 cm−1. The other dopant ratio NCDs produced similar spectra (see Fig. A.1-A.5). The XPS spectra in Fig. 3 support these findings, showing clear peaks for NCD (1:6) at 533 eV (O 1s), 401 eV (N 1s), and 285 eV (C 1s). Expanding the spectra in the O 1s region, two peaks at 531.7 eV and 532.9 eV could be observed after deconvolution, corresponding to O-H and C-O bonds, respectively [20]. Additionally, the two peaks at 401.1 eV and 400.2 eV obtained by deconvoluting the N 1s signal correspond to C-N and N-H bonds. Lastly, three peaks were observed for the C 1s signal at 288.5 eV, 286.1 eV, and 285.1 eV, corresponding to O-C=O, C-O-C, and C-C groups, respectively [21]. Similar results were shown for other NCD ratios (Fig. A. 6-7). As is, all NCD ratios exhibit abundant surface functional groups consistent with hydroxyls, carbonyls, amines, etc. Due to these pH-sensitive functional groups, the NCDs are therefore expected to have excellent water solubility and exhibit pH-dependent fluorescence.

The size distribution and morphology of the NCDs were characterized by TEM (Fig 4 ). The NCDs were mostly spherical or quasi-spherical in shape, ranging in size based on dopant ratio. Based on a statistical analysis of more than 100 dots (see Fig. A.8), the average size of NCD (1:3) was 5.7 ± 3.2 nm, NCD (1:6) was 8.1 ± 3.3 nm, and NCD (1:10) was 20.8 ± 3.3 nm. The lattice spacing was estimated to be 0.33 nm which corresponds to (006) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon. Additionally, the NCDs exhibited hygroscopicity. Of the NCD samples, NCD (6:1) with the lowest dopant ratio exhibited the largest degree of observed hygroscopicity, with decreasing hygroscopicity as the dopant ratio increased to NCD (1:10). This is likely due to increased N dopant content and therefore less hygroscopicity as with the relatively low hygroscopicity of urea. NCDs of lower dopant ratios were more hygroscopic while higher dopant ratios had drier, less observed hygroscopic particles.

The absorbance spectrum of NCD (1:6) in pH 7 (Fig. 5 a) has a peak absorbance at ∼330 nm which is characteristic of citric acid-based carbon dots and attributed to an n to π* transition. Additionally, NCD (1:6) exhibits excitation-dependent fluorescence (see Fig. 5b) with a maximum intensity when excited at 340 nm. The emission gradually redshifts as the excitation wavelength increases. Similar results are shown in literature [22]. The absorbance and emission spectra for NCD (1:1), (1:3), (1:6), and (1:10) were largely similar (see Fig. A.10-A.13). Therefore, altering the carbon to dopant ratios has little effect on maximum absorbance or emission wavelengths, similar to other reports. However, there is an observed change in visible color and fluorescence intensity between species, especially upon dilution.

Fig. 6 shows a comparison of NCD (6:1), (1:1), (1:3), (1:6), and (1:10) in visible light and under 365 nm light at decreasing concentrations. Of interest, NCD (6:1) exhibits almost no color at 1000 ppm or below. At higher concentrations (∼2000 ppm, not pictured), a slight orange-red color is exhibited at pH 7. The NCD (6:1) despite little to no visible color in white light at or below 1000 ppm, still fluoresces under UV-light (365 nm) at 1000 ppm concentrations with diminishing fluorescence upon dilution (see the first set of Fig. 6). Of the prepared NCDs, NCD (6:1) exhibits the least fluorescence intensity per same concentration, likely due to lesser degree of doping via urea, and therefore lesser fluorescence tuning. At present, the NCD (1:1) pictured in the second set of Fig. 6 is dark purple at 1000 ppm, becoming lighter purple upon dilution. NCD (1:3), shown in the third set of Fig. 6, shows a gray-brown colored solution becoming lighter upon dilution. NCD (1:6) are orange-brown colored solutions, becoming yellow upon dilution. As with previous NCDs, the NCD (1:6) has the most observed fluorescence at 100 ppm, with little to no fluorescence at 1 ppm. Finally, NCD (1:10) exhibited a brown-colored solution at 1000 ppm which became more yellow-green upon dilution. Additionally, the observed fluorescence appears highest at 100 ppm, though the 1 ppm still shows some fluorescence indicating the potentially larger fluorescent QY of NCD (1:10) than NCD (1:6). Although all NCD fluoresce blue at pH 7 at all concentrations, all except NCD (6:1) show the greatest observed fluorescence at 100 ppm. Higher concentrations of 500 ppm and 1000 ppm equivalents show less fluorescence likely due to inner filter effects. All NCDs have little to no fluorescence at 1 ppm.

As NCDs have various surface functional groups including carboxyl and amine, they are sensitive to changing pH environments. Herein, the pH-dependent fluorescence is presented for NCD (6:1) through (1:10) at pH 1-13. Of note, inset images show the same concentration equivalents of each NCD sample in pH 2, 7, and 13 under visible and UV light.

NCD (6:1) fluorescence is greatly increased from acidic to basic media, with pH 11 being the highest fluorescing (Fig. 7 ). This has been attributed to decreasing energy loss from “non-radiative recombination” made possible by the less positive (and later more negative) surface shell formed from the deprotonation of carboxylic acid and amino groups from acidic to basic media [2]. The observed fluorescence in the leftmost inset images in Fig. 7 shows only slight fluorescence at 100 ppm in pH 11 and both pH 2 and 7 have no observable fluorescence at that low of a concentration.

The emission spectra of NCD (1:1) from pH 1-13, shown in Fig. 8 , follows a general increase in fluorescence with increasingly basic media (see rightmost inset image in Fig. 8). Notably, NCD (1:1) is purple at pH 7. At pH 11, the color is light yellow, and at pH 2, a light orange. This is visually confirmed by the larger fluorescence intensity in pH 7 and pH 11 of Fig. 8’s leftmost inset images, than that of pH 2.

Fig. 9 shows the emission spectra of NCD (1:3) from pH 1-13. As with lower dopant ratio NCDs, NCD (1:3) follows a similar trend of increased fluorescence intensity from acidic to basic media (see rightmost inset image in Fig. 9) with the highest fluorescence at pH 10. The leftmost inset images in Fig. 8 show brown coloring at pH 2, gray at pH 7, and yellow at pH 2. Similarly, the fluorescence at pH 7 and pH 11 look largely the same in the inset image in Fig. 9, though, unlike previous NCD counterparts, the pH 2 NCD (1:6) shows some fluorescence despite comparative concentrations. This demonstrates the stark fluorescent capability of higher dopant ratio NCDs in comparison to lower dopant ratio NCDs at the same concentration. Based on these results, the QY is expected to increase as the dopant ratio increases.

Similarly, NCD (1:6) exhibited an increased fluorescence intensity with increasingly basic media, as shown in Fig. 10 and in the rightmost inset image. The leftmost inset images in Fig. 10 show a slight change in color from pH 7 (yellow) to pH 2 (orange). The pH 11 closely resembled pH 7 in color under white light. As with previous NCDs, fluorescence intensity was largely dependent on pH, whereby the fluorescence is highest in basic media from pH 7-11.
Finally, NCD (1:10) likewise exhibits increased fluorescence intensity in increasingly basic media, as shown in the emission spectra of NCD (1:10) in pH 1-12 in Fig. 11 .

The QY of NCD (6:1), (1:1), (1:3), (1:6), and (1:10) were calculated for buffered pH 2, 5, 7, 11, and 13 solutions without further purification. The QYs were calculated in reference to quinine sulfate and the results are tabulated in Fig. 12 . Of the five CD samples, NCD (6:1) has the lowest molar ratio of urea dopant. Likewise, it also has the lowest overall QYs, exhibiting an overall decrease in QY with increases in pH from 2-13. Its highest QY is in pH 2 (4.22%) and lowest in pH 7 (1.40%). Unsurprisingly, NCD (1:1) had an overall increased QY from that of NCD (6:1), largely due to increased NH2 groups from an increased urea dopant ratio. The highest QY was in pH 11 (14.10%) while the lowest was in pH 2 (0.89%). NCD (1:3) has the highest QY in pH 11 (6.56%) and the lowest in pH 2 (1.10%). NCD (1:6) has the highest QY of all prepared CDs, making its composition the best choice. The highest QY is at pH 11 (19.18%) and the lowest QY is at pH 2 (0.60%). Finally, though the NCD (1:10) was theorized to have an even higher overall QY due to the largest amount of urea dopant, it is only slightly above NCD (1:3). The highest QY is at pH 13 (9.94%) and lowest at pH 2 (0.81%).

Overall, NCDs exhibit lower QYs in acidic media and higher QYs in basic media. Except for NCD (6:1), NCDs exhibit only slightly lower fluorescence in pH 7 than in basic media. Therefore, to avoid harsher chemicals required for higher alkaline environments, a small decrease in QY can be traded for mild, neutral conditions.

Overall, NCDs of varying carbon-to-dopant molar ratios were synthesized to determine the best composition for increased QY. Of all NCD ratios, NCD (1:6) had the highest QY of 19% in pH 11. Each NCD sample demonstrated pH-dependent fluorescence with an overall trend of increased fluorescence intensity in increasingly basic pHs. Though the intensity and QY were largely similar between pH 7-13, these results indicate that a small decrease in QY output is a good trade-off for more neutral media of pH 7-10. Of the NCDs, NCD (6:1), with the lowest N dopant ratio, demonstrated the lowest QY, further supporting the positive effect doping with N has on achievable QY. As is, the NCDs as prepared may prove useful in future applications in pH sensing or bioimaging.

Megan B. Prado: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Nguyen T. Truong: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Adam K. Wanekaya: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare they have no known competing financial or personal interests that may have influenced the work in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the Missouri State University Graduate College and the Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry. Additional support by the Harthcock Chemistry Fellowship (to Megan Prado).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.snr.2023.100165.

C. 3 (2015) 5976–5984. https://doi.org/10.1039/C5TC00813A.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as fluorescent probe for detection of curcumin based on the inner filter effect
RSC Adv.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Zhang, C. Zhang, Z. Li, J. Ge, C. Li, C. Dong, S. Shuang, Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as fluorescent probe for detection of curcumin based on the inner filter effect, RSC Adv.. 5 (2015) 95054–95060. https://doi.org/10.1039/C5RA18176C.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Megan B. Prado studied chemistry at Missouri State University and received her bachelor's degree with distinction in 2020. She recently graduated in 2022 with her master's degree in chemistry from Missouri State University with a focus in analytical chemistry. Her master's thesis was on improving the quantum yield of doped carbon dots.
Nguyen T. Truong (Joe) is pursuing his bachelor's degree in chemistry at Missouri State University. He is an aspiring inorganic chemist with a deep passion for blow torches.

Adam K. Wanekaya is currently Professor and Department Head in the Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry at Missouri State University in Springfield, Missouri. He received his Ph.D. in Chemistry from the State University of New York in Binghamton, New York in 2004. He did his Postdoctoral Research at the University of California's Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering in Riverside, California from 2004 to 2006. Current research in his laboratory focuses on the discovery and basic scientific studies of novel sensing materials. Other research interests include the fabrication, modification, characterization, and application of various materials for sensing and remediation.
